pages.full_text:
Most of the museum was rather heavy-going because our French wasn't good enough. I preferred the Stonehenge museum. If it rains less tomorrow we'll go on a guided tour.
  We found __campsite of the white sand__ — a lovely beach. Crowded. It poured & poured rain. After supper there was an amazing rainbow as the sun came out & the sky cleared. We went for a lovely walk along the beach — a beautiful pink lilac sky. Then we read.
__Tuesday__
Sand everywhere, including in bed. Euch. Feet sticky. I had a shower & bravely washed my hair. Sunshine. It dried quickly.
  At 11.30 we started the English tour of the most westerly of the Carnac sites & the heavens kept opening. Cold & wet. Cold & wet. We had a good guide.
  We looked at the enclosure afterwards [illegible]
pages.notes: Page continues from a previous spread. 'campsite of the white sand' appears underlined in the original. The crossed-out stars/asterisks (***) appear after 'white sand' heading — possibly a rating symbol. Bottom line partially cut off at gutter edge. Carnac refers to the megalithic site in Brittany, France. No handwritten page number visible at top-left corner. Year cannot be determined from this page alone; volume B covers 2003–2019.

pages.full_text:
where farm houses had been built in among the stones, using the stones. There are nearly 3,000 stones left. There used to be double that number. It is reckoned that every house around has a stone in from Carnac. They stood up right [—] local stones [and] an enclosure at the end for worship in each of the 3 sites. Celtic words used. Celts arrived 600 BC (Stones 6,000 BC).
Men = stone.
Men-hir: high Stone
Dol-men: stone table.
Menhirs are sites of the living.
Afterwards back in van, set off east. Sunny lunch in lay by. Pick blackberries.
pages.notes: Page is decorated with a diagonal line of adhesive arrowhead/flint stickers running from top-right to bottom-left, partially obscuring some text. The text on either side of the diagonal sticker strip has been transcribed as best as possible. The left column of text (beside stickers) reads: 'where farm houses had been built in among the stones, using the stones. There are nearly 3,000 stones left. There used to be double that number. It is reckoned that every house around has a stone in from Carnac. They stood up right [—] local stones'. The right column beside stickers reads: 'an enclosure at the end for worship in each of the 3 sites. Celtic words used. Celts arrived 600 BC (Stones 6,000 BC).' then continuing below the sticker strip. The phrase 'Pick blackberries' at the bottom is partially obscured by arrowhead stickers. Year cannot be determined from this page alone; volume B covers 2003–2019.
